reasoning: The Lean statement adds `[Fintype G]`, requiring the entire ambient group G to be finite, while the informal statement only says "a group G". The implicit premise in "if |H| and |K| are relatively prime" is merely that H and K are finite (so their orders are natural numbers) — NOT that G itself is finite. Mathlib's own proof of this exact result (`Subgroup.inf_eq_bot_of_coprime` in `GroupTheory/Index.lean`) works for arbitrary groups G using `Nat.card` and does not require `[Fintype G]`. Since infinite groups can have finite subgroups with coprime orders, the Lean statement only covers a strict special case of the informal claim: the informal claim implies the Lean claim, but not vice versa. A faithful formalization would use `Nat.card` (or explicit `[Finite H] [Finite K]` instances) instead of `[Fintype G]`.
